---
title: "Pollutant w/ Strongest Effect"
author: "T Smith"
date: "2026-06-30"
output: html_document
---

## I am curious what pollutant has the strongest effect on FEV1. For this i will standardize my predictor variables and attempt to run a multiple linear regression model. Standardizing my pollutanats hsould help allow me to directly compare the effects sizes using the resulting stardized regression coefficients.

```{r}
chs_data <- read.csv("CHS_cohortE(in).csv")
```

```{r compare pollutanta}
## First ill load my libraries needed
library(dplyr)

## Now ill scale my pollutants to standardize them 
analyzed_data <- chs_data %>%
  mutate(
    pm2.5_scaled = scale(pm25),
    elemental.carbon_scaled = scale(ec),
    nitr_scaled = scale(nitrate),
    sulf_scaled = scale(sulfate)
  )

## Now Fit the multiple linear regression model (including any necessary health cofounders like height, age, smoking status)
model <- lm(fev1 ~ pm2.5_scaled + elemental.carbon_scaled
            + sulf_scaled +
              nitr_scaled, data = analyzed_data)
summary(model)

## I'll now see which is the strongest predictor by reading the Estimate column from the summary(model) output. I think the one with largest value is sulfate (-128.18), i may have read it wrong, I think this means its statistically significant. 

## Now I will try to extract the absolute standardized coefficients using:
abs_coefficients <- abs(coef(model)[-1])
strongest_pollutant <- names(which.max(abs_coefficients))
print(strongest_pollutant)
```

```{r plot pollutant effects}
## I want to change my numbers to a visualization, start with libraries:
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(dplyr)

## Extract model coefficients, standardized errors, + confidence intervals
model_results <- tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  
## Exclude the intercept to focus only on the pollutants
filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  
## Giving pollutants better names for charts axis
  mutate(
    term = case_when(
      term == "pm25_scaled" ~ "PM2.5",
      term == "elemental.carbon_scaled" ~ "Elemental Carbon",
      term == "sulf_scaled" ~ "Sulfate",
      term == "nitr_scaled" ~"Nitrate",
      TRUE ~ term
    )
  )
  
## Now I will attempt to create a Forest Plot
ggplot(model_results, aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(term, abs(estimate)))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) + 
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high, height = 0.2, color = "darkblue", linewidth = 1, orientation = "y")) +                                 geom_point(color = "darkblue", size = 4) + 
                   labs(                                                               title = "Effects of Standardized Pollutants on FEV1",
                                                                                       subtitle = "Ordered from strongest effect to weakest",
                                                                                       x = "Standardized Regression Coefficient (Beta Estimate)", 
                                                                                       y = "Pollutant"
                                                                                     ) +
                                                                                    theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
                                                                                    theme(
                    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
                    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),                        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
)
```
